title: The Song of the Red Ruby
description: A novel of sexuality, guilt, youth, and postwar Scandinavian modernity.
descriptionParagraphs: A novel of sexuality, guilt, youth, and postwar Scandinavian modernity.

The Song of the Red Ruby is usually read through its treatment of sexuality, youth, and modernity. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes sexuality, youth, and modernity feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A novel of sexuality, guilt, youth, and postwar Scandinavian modernity.
banningParagraphs: The Song of the Red Ruby entered censorship debates as a novel associated with sexuality, youth, and modernity. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and sexual explicitness.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1950s in Norway, where Norwegian prosecutors prosecuted for obscenity. The novel became the center of a famous Scandinavian obscenity case. Its legal battle reshaped the limits of literary candor in Norway.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
